desc: Siena is one of the few Italian city breaks where the famous square is only the surface layer. The shell of Piazza del Campo, the striped Duomo, the weight of contrada identity and the city's brick-and-stone medieval fabric make it feel ceremonial, proud and unusually self-contained even by Tuscan standards.

todo.name: Piazza del Campo
todo.desc: Start in the shell-shaped square, then climb Torre del Mangia if visibility and legs allow.

todo.name: Siena Cathedral
todo.desc: Book ahead for the Duomo complex, especially when the marble floor is uncovered.

todo.name: Contrada museum visit
todo.desc: Learn how neighbourhood identity shapes the Palio far beyond the race days.

todo.name: Chianti or Val d'Orcia day trip
todo.desc: Use Siena as a base for vineyards, hill towns and cypress-lined roads.

todo.name: Evening Campo circuit
todo.desc: Stay in the centre after day-trippers thin out, when Siena feels less like a museum stop and more like a lived-in city.

todo.name: Santa Maria della Scala or civic museum
todo.desc: Give Siena indoor time as well as skyline views so the city's political and religious history actually lands.

wifi.note: Usually reliable in hotels, apartments and cafes, but the old-city building stock can make signal strength inconsistent room to room and the bigger practical issue is navigating the car-restricted centre, not staying online.

prac.fltNote: Siena has no airport. Pisa is often the simplest Gatwick gateway, while Florence is geographically tidier if the air options suit you; Rome Fiumicino is workable but usually the long-way-round choice. The real planning issue is not the flight but Palio and high-summer Tuscany pressure, when rooms and trains tighten quickly.
prac.lang: Italian; English is common in larger visitor areas
prac.visa: Schengen rules apply; UK/Irish travellers should verify 90-in-180-day limits and passport validity before travel.
alerts: Palio periods require very early booking and crowd tolerance.
The historic centre is hilly and mostly car-restricted.
Summer afternoons are exposed on stone streets.

sweet: Late April to June and September to October are usually Siena's best windows: the city is lively, the countryside works beautifully as a side trip, and you dodge some of the hardest midsummer heat and Palio-driven pressure.
